speaker: interviewer
text: September 30, 1996. We're continuing with the interview of Joyce Lipper. This is tape number five. Tell me who's in this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Starting from the right side, in the back is my mother standing, my father with my two sisters. My grandmother-- that's the one that was in America when I came here. In the back of her is her daughter, my aunt, with her husband and two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Etel and Icak Gelfenbaum is their name. I'm not in the picture yet. I wasn't born. This picture was taken in 1924 and sent to the family in-- in America. And that's how I got all my European pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Klewan, in-- the town that I come from in Poland. This picture was taken in front of our house in Klewan in the early '30s, I would say. This is my mother, my older sister, Shifra, my middle sister, Minna. And I'm in the back there. My name is Joyce. This is my two cousins, two boys, Monye and Sule Gelfenbaum. That's the sister of my father's two children, my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken also in Klewan, in front of their house. And that picture was also sent to the family in America, and that's how I got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these two survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. That's-- this is two brothers from Yankele. He was the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, maybe, I would say in the late '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. This is my oldest sister, Shifra. This was taken in the '30s. She was old enough, and they were making out papers for her to come to America. And then the war started. This was also taken in Klewan in the '30s. This is the youngest son of my aunt, the brother to the two boys that we showed before. And I brought up that the little boy. [INAUDIBLE] mother. And he was killed with his two brothers. At-- not at the same time, but also during the war. He was a youngster.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Yankel. [? Yankela, ?] used to call him. And the picture was taken in Klewan by [INAUDIBLE], and sent to the family in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: His last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gelfenbaum. And I got it from my grandmother [INAUDIBLE] in America. This picture is of my father-in-law, the father of my husband, Eli Lipper. And this is Moishe Lipper, also taken in Europe, in Klewan, and gotten here from the family here in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say in the '30s, late '30s, maybe early '40s. This picture was given to me by my so-called friends in the-- in our town in the Ukraine. They took them themselves. They said, and that was during the extermination of Jews that the German soldiers looked over and the Jews buried some-- the other people. And then they were buried. Yes. And the people thought that I would-- might want to have it, so they gave it to me in '44 when I came back to our town. This is one of the two pictures that I got when I came back to our town. And they gave it to me. They thought that I would want to have it as a remembrance. It shows that the people that they got [? to ghetto ?] and told them to get undressed and get ready to be shot, buried, or whatever they told them. I really don't know. But they thought that I might want to have them. And I kept them and look at them once in a while to remember, to make sure that I don't forget what went on. Those are people from our town. Nobody that I see that I know there, that I can see, but it's a picture that was taken by a non-Jewish person. This was the first picture that we took in Austria after the war that we sent to America, to our family, when we got in touch with them to show them who we are and that we're interested in help coming to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, myself, and my oldest daughter. Was about a year old or so. It was about 1946 or so. And it was made in Bad Gastein, where we were in Austria before we came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in '63 when my husband and I visited Israel. And we got together with some of the people that we were for the 18 months in hiding with them. And now they all gone. Nobody is left from those five people.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left is who?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- on the left standing is my husband, Eli Lipper. And the other lady is Mishka Kanarek. And this is my-- my cousin, Abraham, with his wife. And this is the-- Munya Kuperberg sitting on the right hand side. This is four of them that were in the hiding with us together. I took that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This in 1963 when we visited Israel. We were there for the holiday Sukkot. And my husband, Eli Lipper, with his cousin, Abraham, that we were together in hiding went to the Wailing Wall and [INAUDIBLE]. That was in 1963. Both of them are gone. This picture is my sister-in-law, the one that put her life at risk to save our lives with our cousin, Abraham, in Israel. That was taken in 1963. That was our first visit to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Bella Lipper now. She's the-- a convert. She's-- she's more Jewish than I am. My cousin is gone already. She's still alive. This picture was taken maybe in '48, beginning of '48. We were still in Austria. We had the papers all ready to come to America. But we were still in Austria taking pictures every time to send to the family to America. To-- they should get to know us a little bit. I think that winter we came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's my husband, Eli Lipper. Our daughter, that time Ann Lipper Dean. And myself, Joyce Lipper. This is my older daughter, Ann, with her husband, Joey. They live now in New Jersey. She's a school teacher. He works in-- in a builder-- by a building contractor, with a building contractor. They have no children. They both live in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken about three, four years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, yes. This is my younger daughter, Sharon, with her husband, Bill, Bill Scheele. They-- the picture was taken a couple months after their marriage in '84. They live now in Houston, Texas. This is my two grandchildren, the children of Sharon Scheele and Bill. Evan is 10, and Kandell is eight-- pardon me, seven. And they live in Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken about a year ago, last year before Hanukkah. I got the picture for Hanukkah. That was my Hanukkah present.
